[[ja:御塩殿神社]] [[sn:Mishioden Shrine]] [[sn:ishioden Shrine]]
<!--Mishioden Shrine-->

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Mishioden-jinja_02.JPG|256px|Mishioden Shrine]] | name = Mishioden Shrine (御塩殿神社) | location = Futami-chō Shō, [[Ise, Mie]] | deity = Mishioden Chinju-no-Kami | Rank = {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft}} subsidiary shrine | established = Before [[804]] | main_hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | festival = Mishioden Festival }}
'''Mishioden Shrine''' (御塩殿神社) is a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Futami-chō, Mie|ja|二見町 (三重県)|qq=draft}}, [[Ise, Mie]]. The shrine houses facilities for producing hardened salt (*katashio*), which is an essential offering in Shinto rituals. Within the Ise Grand Shrine (*Jingū*), the official reading of the shrine's name is "Mishiodono," while locals in Futami refer to it as "Mishioden."
== Overview ==
[[File:Mishioden-jinja_10.JPG|thumb|240px|Mishioden Shrine grounds]] '''Mishioden Shrine''' is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft}} that includes facilities for producing hardened salt used in the sacred food offerings (*shinsen*) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft}}. It is located in Futami-chō Shō, Ise, Mie, covering an area of 27,785 [[square meters]] (*m²*). The name "Shō" originates from *Futami-no-Shō*, a land developed for the Jingū during the [[Heian period]].
Originally, the shrine had no formal structure, and the deity was enshrined within the '''Mishioden''', a facility where salt was processed.
Outside the shrine precincts, to the west of Futami Town, lies the '''Mishiohama salt field''' (*御塩浜*), while within the shrine grounds are facilities such as the '''Mishio-Kumiire-sho''' (*御塩汲入所*), '''Mishio-Yakisho''' (*御塩焼所*), '''Mishio-Mikura''' (*御塩御倉*), and '''Mishioden''' (*御塩殿*). These facilities are maintained through the labor of local residents.
Although Mishioden Shrine is under the jurisdiction of the '''Inner Shrine''' (*Naikū*), it also supplies hardened salt for use in rituals at the '''Outer Shrine''' (*Gekū*). One theory suggests that a historical mix-up with {{ill|Katada Shrine|ja|堅田神社|qq=draft}} led to the Gekū receiving salt from Mishioden.
Historically, salt production flourished along the '''Ise Bay''' coastline from Futami-chō to {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)|qq=draft}}, but it declined with the rise of '''irihama-style salt fields''' in the Seto Inland Sea. Today, Mishiohama is the last remaining salt field in this region.
In addition to Mishioden Shrine, other shrines classified as '''Imperial Estate Shrines''' (*Goryōchi shrines*) responsible for producing ritual offerings include the {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|ja|機殿神社|lt='''Kanfusoki Hatadono Shrine'''|lt_ja=神服織機殿神社|qq=draft}} (*神服織機殿神社*), which weaves silk, and the {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|ja|機殿神社|lt='''Kanmisoki Hatadono Shrine'''|lt_ja=神麻続機殿神社|qq=draft}} (*神麻続機殿神社*), which produces hemp fabric. Both are located in {{ill|Matsusaka, Mie|ja|松阪市|qq=draft}}.
== Mishiohama ==
[[File:Mishiohama 01.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishiohama salt field]] [[File:Isuzu-gawa 01.JPG|thumb|160px|Isuzu River near Mishiohama]] [[File:Mishiohama 02.JPG|thumb|160px|Water gate at Mishiohama]]
The '''Mishiohama salt field''' (*御塩浜*) is a 6,609 *m²* salt field associated with the shrine. Although it is not a shrine itself, it features a '''blackwood torii gate'''.
Originally, salt was produced by '''boiling seawater''' since salt-field technology had not yet developed in Japan. After '''salt-field techniques''' became established, the field was constructed in western Futami Town as an '''agehama-style salt field''', though the exact date of its establishment is unknown. Later, it was converted into an '''irihama-style salt field'''.
Due to '''coastal erosion''' and '''land subsidence''', maintenance became difficult, and in 1751 (*Kan'en 4*), embankment work was carried out to preserve the field. However, as maintaining the original location was still challenging, the salt field was moved to its '''current location near the Isuzu River estuary'''.
During the '''Meiji Restoration''', the new government confiscated Mishiohama under the '''Land Requisition Order''', ceasing salt production. However, in 1877 (*Meiji 10*), the Jingū purchased the land, and in 1897 (*Meiji 30*), salt production resumed.
== Mishio-Kumiire-sho ==
[[File:Mishioden-jinja_05.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishio-Kumiire-sho]] The '''Mishio-Kumiire-sho''' (*御塩汲入所*) is a storage facility for brine (*kansui*), which is transported from Mishiohama in barrels. It is located in the eastern part of the shrine grounds near the coast.
The structure is built in the '''Tenchi-Kongen style''' (*天地根元造*), once thought to represent a '''primitive dwelling style''', but later identified as a '''post-classical invention''' rather than an ancient form.
== Mishio-Yakisho ==
[[File:Mishioden-jinja_04.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishio-Yakisho]] The '''Mishio-Yakisho''' (*御塩焼所*) is where the '''brine is boiled in iron kettles''' to produce '''crude salt''' (*arashio*). It is slightly larger than the Kumiire-sho and shares the same '''Tenchi-Kongen construction'''. This process, using '''ritual fire''' (*imibi*), occurs in '''August'''.
== Mishio-Mikura ==
[[File:Mishioden-jinja_08.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishio-Mikura]] The '''Mishio-Mikura''' (*御塩御倉*) is a storage warehouse where '''crude salt is kept''' before processing in the Mishioden.
== Mishioden ==
[[File:Mishio-den-jinja_01.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishioden]] The '''Mishioden''' (*御塩殿*) is the '''facility where hardened salt is made''' by '''firing the crude salt''' in triangular '''earthenware molds''' (*doki*). The process uses ritual fire (*imibi*) and is performed twice a year in '''October and March'''.
== Mishio-michi ==
The '''Mishio-michi''' (*御塩道*) is the designated route for '''transporting the hardened salt''' from Mishioden Shrine to the Jingū. The historical route passed through modern locations such as Futami Town, Kurose-chō, and Kawazaki, before reaching the '''Outer Shrine''' (*Gekū*). Originally carried by hand, transportation switched to '''vehicles''' in the '''Shōwa era'''.
== deity ==
The shrine deity is '''Mishioden Chinju-no-Kami''', though some theories suggest it may originally have been {{ill|Shiotsuchi-no-Oji|ja|シオツチノオジ|qq=draft}}.
== History == The exact origins are unclear, but the ''{{ill|Enryaku Gishikichō|ja|延暦儀式帳|qq=draft}}'' from 804 (Enryaku 23) mentions the {{ill|Mishiodono|ja|御塩殿|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Mishioyaki-dono|ja|御塩焼殿|qq=draft}}. During the 10th–11th centuries in the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft}}, the area around Mishiodono was developed into the {{ill|Futami-no-shō|ja|二見庄|qq=draft}}, which became part of the outer shrine’s territory (*shinryō*, or Gekū domain).
From 1486 (Bunmei 18) during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft}} until the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft}}, Futami was under the control of the warrior class, making it difficult to manage Mishiodono. In the early Edo period, the {{ill|Kuki clan|ja|九鬼家|qq=draft}}, lords of the {{ill|Toba Domain|ja|鳥羽藩|qq=draft}}, faced internal succession disputes, leading to their dispossession. In 1632 (Kan'ei 10), Futami was officially restored as shrine territory (*shinryō*).
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft}}, the new government issued the '''{{ill|Edict for the Confiscation of Shrine and Temple Lands|ja|上知令|qq=draft}}''' in January 1871 (Meiji 4), confiscating all shrine and temple lands except for their immediate precincts, effectively abolishing the shrine land system. As a result, Mishiohama was confiscated, and the production of coarse salt ceased. The {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|神宮|qq=draft}} had to purchase coarse salt from other sources, which was then roasted and hardened at Mishiodono for ritual use.
In 1877 (Meiji 10), the shrine repurchased the land of Mishiohama, and by 1897 (Meiji 30), the traditional production of sacred salt (*mishio chōshin*) resumed at Mishiohama and {{ill|Mishioden Shrine|ja|御塩殿神社|qq=draft}}. The tradition of having former shrine domain residents participate in the salt-making process has been preserved ever since.
== festival ==
The '''Mishioden Festival''' (*御塩殿祭*) is held annually on '''October 5th''', praying for '''successful salt production''' and the '''safety of workers'''.
== Transportation ==
* '''Train''': 1.2 km from {{ill|Futamiura Station|ja|二見浦駅|qq=draft}} * '''Bus''': 900m from the {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft}} Futamiura stop * '''Car''': 2.3 km from the {{ill|Ise Futami Toba Toll Road|ja|伊勢二見鳥羽有料道路|qq=draft}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Shinsen (food offerings)|ja|神饌|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Salt field|ja|塩田|qq=draft}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Futami, Mie|ja|二見町 (三重県)|qq=draft}}
== 交通 == * 最寄駅：[[東海旅客鉄道|JR東海]][[参宮線]][[二見浦駅]]から約1.2km。 * 最寄バス停：[[三重交通]]二見浦表参道バス停下車約900m。 * 最寄インターチェンジ：[[伊勢二見鳥羽有料道路]][[二見TB|二見料金所]]から約2.3km。 * 駐車場：二見グラウンド東隣の二見浦海水浴場の駐車場より徒歩約400m、二見生涯学習センター裏の駐車場より徒歩約800m。
== 参考資料 == {{No footnotes|date=2019th year 10月|section=1}} * 『二見町史』（昭和63rd year 3月31日発行、編集：二見町史編纂委員会、発行：二見町役場） * 『伊勢神宮の祖型と展開』 （平成3rd year 11月30日発行、桜井勝之進著、国書刊行会発行、ISBN 4-336-03296-3） * 『お伊勢まいり』（平成18th year 7月1日発行改訂7版、伊勢神宮崇敬会）
*

[[File:Mishiohama 01.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishiohama salt field]] [[File:Isuzu-gawa 01.JPG|thumb|160px|Isuzu River near Mishiohama]] [[File:Mishiohama 02.JPG|thumb|160px|Water gate at Mishiohama]]

After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft}}, the new government issued the '''{{ill|Edict for the Confiscation of Shrine and Temple Lands|ja|上知令|qq=draft}}''' in January 1871 (Meiji 4), confiscating all shrine and temple lands except for their immediate precincts, effectively abolishing the shrine land system. As a result, Mishiohama was confiscated, and the production of coarse salt ceased. The {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|神宮|qq=draft}} had to purchase coarse salt from other sources, which was then roasted and hardened at Mishiodono for ritual use.
In 1877 (Meiji 10), the shrine repurchased the land of Mishiohama, and by 1897 (Meiji 30), the traditional production of sacred salt (*mishio chōshin*) resumed at Mishiohama and {{ill|Mishioden Shrine|ja|御塩殿神社|qq=draft}}. The tradition of having former shrine domain residents participate in the salt-making process has been preserved ever since.
== festival ==
The '''Mishioden Festival''' (*御塩殿祭*) is held annually on '''October 5th''', praying for '''successful salt production''' and the '''safety of workers'''.
== Transportation ==
* '''Train''': 1.2 km from {{ill|Futamiura Station|ja|二見浦駅|qq=draft}} * '''Bus''': 900m from the {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft}} Futamiura stop * '''Car''': 2.3 km from the {{ill|Ise Futami Toba Toll Road|ja|伊勢二見鳥羽有料道路|qq=draft}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Shinsen (food offerings)|ja|神饌|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Salt field|ja|塩田|qq=draft}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Futami, Mie|ja|二見町 (三重県)|qq=draft}}
== 交通 == * 最寄駅：[[東海旅客鉄道|JR東海]][[参宮線]][[二見浦駅]]から約1.2km。 * 最寄バス停：[[三重交通]]二見浦表参道バス停下車約900m。 * 最寄インターチェンジ：[[伊勢二見鳥羽有料道路]][[二見TB|二見料金所]]から約2.3km。 * 駐車場：二見グラウンド東隣の二見浦海水浴場の駐車場より徒歩約400m、二見生涯学習センター裏の駐車場より徒歩約800m。

*

[[ja:御塩殿神社]] [[sn:Mishioden Shrine]] [[sn:ishioden Shrine]]

revision.comment: Bot: add simplewiki draft link to ill templates
revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Mishioden-jinja_02.JPG|256px|Mishioden Shrine]] | name = Mishioden Shrine (御塩殿神社) | location = Futami-chō Shō, [[Ise, Mie]] | deity = Mishioden Chinju-no-Kami | Rank = {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} subsidiary shrine | established = Before [[804]] | main_hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | festival = Mishioden Festival }}
'''Mishioden Shrine''' (御塩殿神社) is a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} located in {{ill|Futami-chō, Mie|ja|二見町 (三重県)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futami-chō, Mie|qq=}}, [[Ise, Mie]]. The shrine houses facilities for producing hardened salt (*katashio*), which is an essential offering in Shinto rituals. Within the Ise Grand Shrine (*Jingū*), the official reading of the shrine's name is "Mishiodono," while locals in Futami refer to it as "Mishioden."
== Overview ==
[[File:Mishioden-jinja_10.JPG|thumb|240px|Mishioden Shrine grounds]] '''Mishioden Shrine''' is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} that includes facilities for producing hardened salt used in the sacred food offerings (*shinsen*) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}. It is located in Futami-chō Shō, Ise, Mie, covering an area of 27,785 [[square meters]] (*m²*). The name "Shō" originates from *Futami-no-Shō*, a land developed for the Jingū during the [[Heian period]].
Originally, the shrine had no formal structure, and the deity was enshrined within the '''Mishioden''', a facility where salt was processed.
Outside the shrine precincts, to the west of Futami Town, lies the '''Mishiohama salt field''' (*御塩浜*), while within the shrine grounds are facilities such as the '''Mishio-Kumiire-sho''' (*御塩汲入所*), '''Mishio-Yakisho''' (*御塩焼所*), '''Mishio-Mikura''' (*御塩御倉*), and '''Mishioden''' (*御塩殿*). These facilities are maintained through the labor of local residents.
Although Mishioden Shrine is under the jurisdiction of the '''Inner Shrine''' (*Naikū*), it also supplies hardened salt for use in rituals at the '''Outer Shrine''' (*Gekū*). One theory suggests that a historical mix-up with {{ill|Katada Shrine|ja|堅田神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katada Shrine|qq=}} led to the Gekū receiving salt from Mishioden.
Historically, salt production flourished along the '''Ise Bay''' coastline from Futami-chō to {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōminato, Ise|qq=}}, but it declined with the rise of '''irihama-style salt fields''' in the Seto Inland Sea. Today, Mishiohama is the last remaining salt field in this region.
In addition to Mishioden Shrine, other shrines classified as '''Imperial Estate Shrines''' (*Goryōchi shrines*) responsible for producing ritual offerings include the {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|ja|機殿神社|lt='''Kanfusoki Hatadono Shrine'''|lt_ja=神服織機殿神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatadono Shrines|qq=}} (*神服織機殿神社*), which weaves silk, and the {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|ja|機殿神社|lt='''Kanmisoki Hatadono Shrine'''|lt_ja=神麻続機殿神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatadono Shrines|qq=}} (*神麻続機殿神社*), which produces hemp fabric. Both are located in {{ill|Matsusaka, Mie|ja|松阪市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsusaka, Mie|qq=}}.
== Mishiohama ==
[[File:Mishiohama 01.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishiohama salt field]] [[File:Isuzu-gawa 01.JPG|thumb|160px|Isuzu River near Mishiohama]] [[File:Mishiohama 02.JPG|thumb|160px|Water gate at Mishiohama]]
The '''Mishiohama salt field''' (*御塩浜*) is a 6,609 *m²* salt field associated with the shrine. Although it is not a shrine itself, it features a '''blackwood torii gate'''.
Originally, salt was produced by '''boiling seawater''' since salt-field technology had not yet developed in Japan. After '''salt-field techniques''' became established, the field was constructed in western Futami Town as an '''agehama-style salt field''', though the exact date of its establishment is unknown. Later, it was converted into an '''irihama-style salt field'''.
Due to '''coastal erosion''' and '''land subsidence''', maintenance became difficult, and in 1751 (*Kan'en 4*), embankment work was carried out to preserve the field. However, as maintaining the original location was still challenging, the salt field was moved to its '''current location near the Isuzu River estuary'''.
During the '''Meiji Restoration''', the new government confiscated Mishiohama under the '''Land Requisition Order''', ceasing salt production. However, in 1877 (*Meiji 10*), the Jingū purchased the land, and in 1897 (*Meiji 30*), salt production resumed.
== Mishio-Kumiire-sho ==
[[File:Mishioden-jinja_05.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishio-Kumiire-sho]] The '''Mishio-Kumiire-sho''' (*御塩汲入所*) is a storage facility for brine (*kansui*), which is transported from Mishiohama in barrels. It is located in the eastern part of the shrine grounds near the coast.
The structure is built in the '''Tenchi-Kongen style''' (*天地根元造*), once thought to represent a '''primitive dwelling style''', but later identified as a '''post-classical invention''' rather than an ancient form.
== Mishio-Yakisho ==
[[File:Mishioden-jinja_04.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishio-Yakisho]] The '''Mishio-Yakisho''' (*御塩焼所*) is where the '''brine is boiled in iron kettles''' to produce '''crude salt''' (*arashio*). It is slightly larger than the Kumiire-sho and shares the same '''Tenchi-Kongen construction'''. This process, using '''ritual fire''' (*imibi*), occurs in '''August'''.
== Mishio-Mikura ==
[[File:Mishioden-jinja_08.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishio-Mikura]] The '''Mishio-Mikura''' (*御塩御倉*) is a storage warehouse where '''crude salt is kept''' before processing in the Mishioden.
== Mishioden ==
[[File:Mishio-den-jinja_01.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishioden]] The '''Mishioden''' (*御塩殿*) is the '''facility where hardened salt is made''' by '''firing the crude salt''' in triangular '''earthenware molds''' (*doki*). The process uses ritual fire (*imibi*) and is performed twice a year in '''October and March'''.
== Mishio-michi ==
The '''Mishio-michi''' (*御塩道*) is the designated route for '''transporting the hardened salt''' from Mishioden Shrine to the Jingū. The historical route passed through modern locations such as Futami Town, Kurose-chō, and Kawazaki, before reaching the '''Outer Shrine''' (*Gekū*). Originally carried by hand, transportation switched to '''vehicles''' in the '''Shōwa era'''.
== deity ==
The shrine deity is '''Mishioden Chinju-no-Kami''', though some theories suggest it may originally have been {{ill|Shiotsuchi-no-Oji|ja|シオツチノオジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiotsuchi-no-Oji|qq=}}.
== History == The exact origins are unclear, but the ''{{ill|Enryaku Gishikichō|ja|延暦儀式帳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryaku Gishikichō|qq=}}'' from 804 (Enryaku 23) mentions the {{ill|Mishiodono|ja|御塩殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mishiodono|qq=}} and {{ill|Mishioyaki-dono|ja|御塩焼殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mishioyaki-dono|qq=}}. During the 10th–11th centuries in the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}, the area around Mishiodono was developed into the {{ill|Futami-no-shō|ja|二見庄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futami-no-shō|qq=}}, which became part of the outer shrine’s territory (*shinryō*, or Gekū domain).
From 1486 (Bunmei 18) during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}} until the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, Futami was under the control of the warrior class, making it difficult to manage Mishiodono. In the early Edo period, the {{ill|Kuki clan|ja|九鬼家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuki clan|qq=}}, lords of the {{ill|Toba Domain|ja|鳥羽藩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toba Domain|qq=}}, faced internal succession disputes, leading to their dispossession. In 1632 (Kan'ei 10), Futami was officially restored as shrine territory (*shinryō*).
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}}, the new government issued the '''{{ill|Edict for the Confiscation of Shrine and Temple Lands|ja|上知令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edict for the Confiscation of Shrine and Temple Lands|qq=}}''' in January 1871 (Meiji 4), confiscating all shrine and temple lands except for their immediate precincts, effectively abolishing the shrine land system. As a result, Mishiohama was confiscated, and the production of coarse salt ceased. The {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}} had to purchase coarse salt from other sources, which was then roasted and hardened at Mishiodono for ritual use.
In 1877 (Meiji 10), the shrine repurchased the land of Mishiohama, and by 1897 (Meiji 30), the traditional production of sacred salt (*mishio chōshin*) resumed at Mishiohama and {{ill|Mishioden Shrine|ja|御塩殿神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mishioden Shrine|qq=}}. The tradition of having former shrine domain residents participate in the salt-making process has been preserved ever since.
== festival ==
The '''Mishioden Festival''' (*御塩殿祭*) is held annually on '''October 5th''', praying for '''successful salt production''' and the '''safety of workers'''.
== Transportation ==
* '''Train''': 1.2 km from {{ill|Futamiura Station|ja|二見浦駅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futamiura Station|qq=}} * '''Bus''': 900m from the {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Kotsu|qq=}} Futamiura stop * '''Car''': 2.3 km from the {{ill|Ise Futami Toba Toll Road|ja|伊勢二見鳥羽有料道路|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Futami Toba Toll Road|qq=}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Shinsen (food offerings)|ja|神饌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsen (food offerings)|qq=}} * {{ill|Salt field|ja|塩田|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Salt field|qq=}} * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|qq=}} * {{ill|Futami, Mie|ja|二見町 (三重県)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futami, Mie|qq=}}
== 交通 == * 最寄駅：[[東海旅客鉄道|JR東海]][[参宮線]][[二見浦駅]]から約1.2km。 * 最寄バス停：[[三重交通]]二見浦表参道バス停下車約900m。 * 最寄インターチェンジ：[[伊勢二見鳥羽有料道路]][[二見TB|二見料金所]]から約2.3km。 * 駐車場：二見グラウンド東隣の二見浦海水浴場の駐車場より徒歩約400m、二見生涯学習センター裏の駐車場より徒歩約800m。
== 参考資料 == {{No footnotes|date=2019th year 10月|section=1}} * 『二見町史』（昭和63rd year 3月31日発行、編集：二見町史編纂委員会、発行：二見町役場） * 『伊勢神宮の祖型と展開』 （平成3rd year 11月30日発行、桜井勝之進著、国書刊行会発行、ISBN 4-336-03296-3） * 『お伊勢まいり』（平成18th year 7月1日発行改訂7版、伊勢神宮崇敬会）
*
== External Links == {{commonscat|Mishioden-jinja}} * [http://www.ise-kanko.jp/base_data/base_data.php?info_cd=00336 伊勢市観光協会／御塩殿神社] * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m10/1001.html 御塩殿神社（みしおどのじんじゃ）] - 伊勢神宮崇敬会

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Mishioden-jinja_02.JPG|256px|Mishioden Shrine]] | name = Mishioden Shrine (御塩殿神社) | location = Futami-chō Shō, [[Ise, Mie]] | deity = Mishioden Chinju-no-Kami | Rank = {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} subsidiary shrine | established = Before [[804]] | main_hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | festival = Mishioden Festival }}
'''Mishioden Shrine''' (御塩殿神社) is a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} located in {{ill|Futami-chō, Mie|ja|二見町 (三重県)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futami-chō, Mie|qq=}}, [[Ise, Mie]]. The shrine houses facilities for producing hardened salt (*katashio*), which is an essential offering in Shinto rituals. Within the Ise Grand Shrine (*Jingū*), the official reading of the shrine's name is "Mishiodono," while locals in Futami refer to it as "Mishioden."
== Overview ==
[[File:Mishioden-jinja_10.JPG|thumb|240px|Mishioden Shrine grounds]] '''Mishioden Shrine''' is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} that includes facilities for producing hardened salt used in the sacred food offerings (*shinsen*) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}. One theory suggests that a historical mix-up with {{ill|Katada Shrine|ja|堅田神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katada Shrine|qq=}} led to the Gekū receiving salt from Mishioden.

Both are located in {{ill|Matsusaka, Mie|ja|松阪市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsusaka, Mie|qq=}}.
== Mishiohama ==
[[File:Mishiohama 01.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishiohama salt field]] [[File:Isuzu-gawa 01.JPG|thumb|160px|Isuzu River near Mishiohama]] [[File:Mishiohama 02.JPG|thumb|160px|Water gate at Mishiohama]]
The '''Mishiohama salt field''' (*御塩浜*) is a 6,609 *m²* salt field associated with the shrine. Although it is not a shrine itself, it features a '''blackwood torii gate'''.
Originally, salt was produced by '''boiling seawater''' since salt-field technology had not yet developed in Japan. After '''salt-field techniques''' became established, the field was constructed in western Futami Town as an '''agehama-style salt field''', though the exact date of its establishment is unknown. Later, it was converted into an '''irihama-style salt field'''.
Due to '''coastal erosion''' and '''land subsidence''', maintenance became difficult, and in 1751 (*Kan'en 4*), embankment work was carried out to preserve the field. However, as maintaining the original location was still challenging, the salt field was moved to its '''current location near the Isuzu River estuary'''.
During the '''Meiji Restoration''', the new government confiscated Mishiohama under the '''Land Requisition Order''', ceasing salt production. However, in 1877 (*Meiji 10*), the Jingū purchased the land, and in 1897 (*Meiji 30*), salt production resumed.
== Mishio-Kumiire-sho ==
[[File:Mishioden-jinja_05.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishio-Kumiire-sho]] The '''Mishio-Kumiire-sho''' (*御塩汲入所*) is a storage facility for brine (*kansui*), which is transported from Mishiohama in barrels. It is located in the eastern part of the shrine grounds near the coast.
The structure is built in the '''Tenchi-Kongen style''' (*天地根元造*), once thought to represent a '''primitive dwelling style''', but later identified as a '''post-classical invention''' rather than an ancient form.
== Mishio-Yakisho ==
[[File:Mishioden-jinja_04.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishio-Yakisho]] The '''Mishio-Yakisho''' (*御塩焼所*) is where the '''brine is boiled in iron kettles''' to produce '''crude salt''' (*arashio*). It is slightly larger than the Kumiire-sho and shares the same '''Tenchi-Kongen construction'''. This process, using '''ritual fire''' (*imibi*), occurs in '''August'''.
== Mishio-Mikura ==
[[File:Mishioden-jinja_08.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishio-Mikura]] The '''Mishio-Mikura''' (*御塩御倉*) is a storage warehouse where '''crude salt is kept''' before processing in the Mishioden.
== Mishioden ==
[[File:Mishio-den-jinja_01.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishioden]] The '''Mishioden''' (*御塩殿*) is the '''facility where hardened salt is made''' by '''firing the crude salt''' in triangular '''earthenware molds''' (*doki*). The process uses ritual fire (*imibi*) and is performed twice a year in '''October and March'''.
== Mishio-michi ==
The '''Mishio-michi''' (*御塩道*) is the designated route for '''transporting the hardened salt''' from Mishioden Shrine to the Jingū. The historical route passed through modern locations such as Futami Town, Kurose-chō, and Kawazaki, before reaching the '''Outer Shrine''' (*Gekū*). Originally carried by hand, transportation switched to '''vehicles''' in the '''Shōwa era'''.
== deity ==
The shrine deity is '''Mishioden Chinju-no-Kami''', though some theories suggest it may originally have been {{ill|Shiotsuchi-no-Oji|ja|シオツチノオジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiotsuchi-no-Oji|qq=}}.
== History == The exact origins are unclear, but the ''{{ill|Enryaku Gishikichō|ja|延暦儀式帳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryaku Gishikichō|qq=}}'' from 804 (Enryaku 23) mentions the {{ill|Mishiodono|ja|御塩殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mishiodono|qq=}} and {{ill|Mishioyaki-dono|ja|御塩焼殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mishioyaki-dono|qq=}}. During the 10th–11th centuries in the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}, the area around Mishiodono was developed into the {{ill|Futami-no-shō|ja|二見庄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futami-no-shō|qq=}}, which became part of the outer shrine’s territory (*shinryō*, or Gekū domain).
From 1486 (Bunmei 18) during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}} until the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, Futami was under the control of the warrior class, making it difficult to manage Mishiodono. In the early Edo period, the {{ill|Kuki clan|ja|九鬼家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuki clan|qq=}}, lords of the {{ill|Toba Domain|ja|鳥羽藩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toba Domain|qq=}}, faced internal succession disputes, leading to their dispossession. In 1632 (Kan'ei 10), Futami was officially restored as shrine territory (*shinryō*).
After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}}, the new government issued the '''{{ill|Edict for the Confiscation of Shrine and Temple Lands|ja|上知令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edict for the Confiscation of Shrine and Temple Lands|qq=}}''' in January 1871 (Meiji 4), confiscating all shrine and temple lands except for their immediate precincts, effectively abolishing the shrine land system. As a result, Mishiohama was confiscated, and the production of coarse salt ceased. The {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}} had to purchase coarse salt from other sources, which was then roasted and hardened at Mishiodono for ritual use.
In 1877 (Meiji 10), the shrine repurchased the land of Mishiohama, and by 1897 (Meiji 30), the traditional production of sacred salt (*mishio chōshin*) resumed at Mishiohama and {{ill|Mishioden Shrine|ja|御塩殿神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mishioden Shrine|qq=}}. The tradition of having former shrine domain residents participate in the salt-making process has been preserved ever since.
== festival ==
The '''Mishioden Festival''' (*御塩殿祭*) is held annually on '''October 5th''', praying for '''successful salt production''' and the '''safety of workers'''.
== Transportation ==
* '''Train''': 1.2 km from {{ill|Futamiura Station|ja|二見浦駅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futamiura Station|qq=}} * '''Bus''': 900m from the {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Kotsu|qq=}} Futamiura stop * '''Car''': 2.3 km from the {{ill|Ise Futami Toba Toll Road|ja|伊勢二見鳥羽有料道路|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Futami Toba Toll Road|qq=}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Shinsen (food offerings)|ja|神饌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsen (food offerings)|qq=}} * {{ill|Salt field|ja|塩田|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Salt field|qq=}} * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|qq=}} * {{ill|Futami, Mie|ja|二見町 (三重県)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futami, Mie|qq=}}
== 交通 == * 最寄駅：[[東海旅客鉄道|JR東海]][[参宮線]][[二見浦駅]]から約1.2km。 * 最寄バス停：[[三重交通]]二見浦表参道バス停下車約900m。 * 最寄インターチェンジ：[[伊勢二見鳥羽有料道路]][[二見TB|二見料金所]]から約2.3km。 * 駐車場：二見グラウンド東隣の二見浦海水浴場の駐車場より徒歩約400m、二見生涯学習センター裏の駐車場より徒歩約800m。

[[ja:御塩殿神社]] [[sn:Mishioden Shrine]] [[sn:ishioden Shrine]]

revision.comment: Bot: replace {{ill}} with enwiki link via Wikidata
revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Mishioden-jinja_02.JPG|256px|Mishioden Shrine]] | name = Mishioden Shrine (御塩殿神社) | location = Futami-chō Shō, [[Ise, Mie]] | deity = Mishioden Chinju-no-Kami | Rank = {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} subsidiary shrine | established = Before [[804]] | main_hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | festival = Mishioden Festival }}
'''Mishioden Shrine''' (御塩殿神社) is a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} located in [[:en:Futami, Mie|Futami-chō, Mie]], [[Ise, Mie]]. The shrine houses facilities for producing hardened salt (*katashio*), which is an essential offering in Shinto rituals. Within the Ise Grand Shrine (*Jingū*), the official reading of the shrine's name is "Mishiodono," while locals in Futami refer to it as "Mishioden."
== Overview ==
[[File:Mishioden-jinja_10.JPG|thumb|240px|Mishioden Shrine grounds]] '''Mishioden Shrine''' is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] that includes facilities for producing hardened salt used in the sacred food offerings (*shinsen*) of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]. It is located in Futami-chō Shō, Ise, Mie, covering an area of 27,785 [[square meters]] (*m²*). The name "Shō" originates from *Futami-no-Shō*, a land developed for the Jingū during the [[Heian period]].
Originally, the shrine had no formal structure, and the deity was enshrined within the '''Mishioden''', a facility where salt was processed.
Outside the shrine precincts, to the west of Futami Town, lies the '''Mishiohama salt field''' (*御塩浜*), while within the shrine grounds are facilities such as the '''Mishio-Kumiire-sho''' (*御塩汲入所*), '''Mishio-Yakisho''' (*御塩焼所*), '''Mishio-Mikura''' (*御塩御倉*), and '''Mishioden''' (*御塩殿*). These facilities are maintained through the labor of local residents.
Although Mishioden Shrine is under the jurisdiction of the '''Inner Shrine''' (*Naikū*), it also supplies hardened salt for use in rituals at the '''Outer Shrine''' (*Gekū*). One theory suggests that a historical mix-up with {{ill|Katada Shrine|ja|堅田神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katada Shrine|qq=}} led to the Gekū receiving salt from Mishioden.
Historically, salt production flourished along the '''Ise Bay''' coastline from Futami-chō to {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōminato, Ise|qq=}}, but it declined with the rise of '''irihama-style salt fields''' in the Seto Inland Sea. Today, Mishiohama is the last remaining salt field in this region.
In addition to Mishioden Shrine, other shrines classified as '''Imperial Estate Shrines''' (*Goryōchi shrines*) responsible for producing ritual offerings include the {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|ja|機殿神社|lt='''Kanfusoki Hatadono Shrine'''|lt_ja=神服織機殿神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatadono Shrines|qq=}} (*神服織機殿神社*), which weaves silk, and the {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|ja|機殿神社|lt='''Kanmisoki Hatadono Shrine'''|lt_ja=神麻続機殿神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatadono Shrines|qq=}} (*神麻続機殿神社*), which produces hemp fabric. Both are located in [[:en:Matsusaka|Matsusaka, Mie]].
== Mishiohama ==
[[File:Mishiohama 01.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishiohama salt field]] [[File:Isuzu-gawa 01.JPG|thumb|160px|Isuzu River near Mishiohama]] [[File:Mishiohama 02.JPG|thumb|160px|Water gate at Mishiohama]]
The '''Mishiohama salt field''' (*御塩浜*) is a 6,609 *m²* salt field associated with the shrine. Although it is not a shrine itself, it features a '''blackwood torii gate'''.
Originally, salt was produced by '''boiling seawater''' since salt-field technology had not yet developed in Japan. After '''salt-field techniques''' became established, the field was constructed in western Futami Town as an '''agehama-style salt field''', though the exact date of its establishment is unknown. Later, it was converted into an '''irihama-style salt field'''.
Due to '''coastal erosion''' and '''land subsidence''', maintenance became difficult, and in 1751 (*Kan'en 4*), embankment work was carried out to preserve the field. However, as maintaining the original location was still challenging, the salt field was moved to its '''current location near the Isuzu River estuary'''.
During the '''Meiji Restoration''', the new government confiscated Mishiohama under the '''Land Requisition Order''', ceasing salt production. However, in 1877 (*Meiji 10*), the Jingū purchased the land, and in 1897 (*Meiji 30*), salt production resumed.
== Mishio-Kumiire-sho ==
[[File:Mishioden-jinja_05.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishio-Kumiire-sho]] The '''Mishio-Kumiire-sho''' (*御塩汲入所*) is a storage facility for brine (*kansui*), which is transported from Mishiohama in barrels. It is located in the eastern part of the shrine grounds near the coast.
The structure is built in the '''Tenchi-Kongen style''' (*天地根元造*), once thought to represent a '''primitive dwelling style''', but later identified as a '''post-classical invention''' rather than an ancient form.
== Mishio-Yakisho ==
[[File:Mishioden-jinja_04.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishio-Yakisho]] The '''Mishio-Yakisho''' (*御塩焼所*) is where the '''brine is boiled in iron kettles''' to produce '''crude salt''' (*arashio*). It is slightly larger than the Kumiire-sho and shares the same '''Tenchi-Kongen construction'''. This process, using '''ritual fire''' (*imibi*), occurs in '''August'''.
== Mishio-Mikura ==
[[File:Mishioden-jinja_08.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishio-Mikura]] The '''Mishio-Mikura''' (*御塩御倉*) is a storage warehouse where '''crude salt is kept''' before processing in the Mishioden.
== Mishioden ==
[[File:Mishio-den-jinja_01.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishioden]] The '''Mishioden''' (*御塩殿*) is the '''facility where hardened salt is made''' by '''firing the crude salt''' in triangular '''earthenware molds''' (*doki*). The process uses ritual fire (*imibi*) and is performed twice a year in '''October and March'''.
== Mishio-michi ==
The '''Mishio-michi''' (*御塩道*) is the designated route for '''transporting the hardened salt''' from Mishioden Shrine to the Jingū. The historical route passed through modern locations such as Futami Town, Kurose-chō, and Kawazaki, before reaching the '''Outer Shrine''' (*Gekū*). Originally carried by hand, transportation switched to '''vehicles''' in the '''Shōwa era'''.
== deity ==
The shrine deity is '''Mishioden Chinju-no-Kami''', though some theories suggest it may originally have been {{ill|Shiotsuchi-no-Oji|ja|シオツチノオジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiotsuchi-no-Oji|qq=}}.
== History == The exact origins are unclear, but the ''{{ill|Enryaku Gishikichō|ja|延暦儀式帳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryaku Gishikichō|qq=}}'' from 804 (Enryaku 23) mentions the {{ill|Mishiodono|ja|御塩殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mishiodono|qq=}} and {{ill|Mishioyaki-dono|ja|御塩焼殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mishioyaki-dono|qq=}}. During the 10th–11th centuries in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the area around Mishiodono was developed into the {{ill|Futami-no-shō|ja|二見庄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futami-no-shō|qq=}}, which became part of the outer shrine’s territory (*shinryō*, or Gekū domain).
From 1486 (Bunmei 18) during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] until the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Futami was under the control of the warrior class, making it difficult to manage Mishiodono. In the early Edo period, the {{ill|Kuki clan|ja|九鬼家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuki clan|qq=}}, lords of the [[:en:Toba Domain|Toba Domain]], faced internal succession disputes, leading to their dispossession. In 1632 (Kan'ei 10), Futami was officially restored as shrine territory (*shinryō*).
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the new government issued the '''{{ill|Edict for the Confiscation of Shrine and Temple Lands|ja|上知令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edict for the Confiscation of Shrine and Temple Lands|qq=}}''' in January 1871 (Meiji 4), confiscating all shrine and temple lands except for their immediate precincts, effectively abolishing the shrine land system. As a result, Mishiohama was confiscated, and the production of coarse salt ceased. The [[:en:List of Jingū|Ise Grand Shrine]] had to purchase coarse salt from other sources, which was then roasted and hardened at Mishiodono for ritual use.
In 1877 (Meiji 10), the shrine repurchased the land of Mishiohama, and by 1897 (Meiji 30), the traditional production of sacred salt (*mishio chōshin*) resumed at Mishiohama and {{ill|Mishioden Shrine|ja|御塩殿神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mishioden Shrine|qq=}}. The tradition of having former shrine domain residents participate in the salt-making process has been preserved ever since.
== festival ==
The '''Mishioden Festival''' (*御塩殿祭*) is held annually on '''October 5th''', praying for '''successful salt production''' and the '''safety of workers'''.
== Transportation ==
* '''Train''': 1.2 km from [[:en:Futaminoura Station|Futamiura Station]] * '''Bus''': 900m from the [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] Futamiura stop * '''Car''': 2.3 km from the {{ill|Ise Futami Toba Toll Road|ja|伊勢二見鳥羽有料道路|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Futami Toba Toll Road|qq=}}
== See Also == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] * [[:en:Shinsen|Shinsen (food offerings)]] * [[:en:Salt evaporation pond|Salt field]] * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|qq=}} * [[:en:Futami, Mie|Futami, Mie]]
== 交通 == * 最寄駅：[[東海旅客鉄道|JR東海]][[参宮線]][[二見浦駅]]から約1.2km。 * 最寄バス停：[[三重交通]]二見浦表参道バス停下車約900m。 * 最寄インターチェンジ：[[伊勢二見鳥羽有料道路]][[二見TB|二見料金所]]から約2.3km。 * 駐車場：二見グラウンド東隣の二見浦海水浴場の駐車場より徒歩約400m、二見生涯学習センター裏の駐車場より徒歩約800m。

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Mishioden-jinja_02.JPG|256px|Mishioden Shrine]] | name = Mishioden Shrine (御塩殿神社) | location = Futami-chō Shō, [[Ise, Mie]] | deity = Mishioden Chinju-no-Kami | Rank = {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} subsidiary shrine | established = Before [[804]] | main_hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | festival = Mishioden Festival }}

Both are located in [[:en:Matsusaka|Matsusaka, Mie]].
== Mishiohama ==
[[File:Mishiohama 01.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishiohama salt field]] [[File:Isuzu-gawa 01.JPG|thumb|160px|Isuzu River near Mishiohama]] [[File:Mishiohama 02.JPG|thumb|160px|Water gate at Mishiohama]]
The '''Mishiohama salt field''' (*御塩浜*) is a 6,609 *m²* salt field associated with the shrine. Although it is not a shrine itself, it features a '''blackwood torii gate'''.
Originally, salt was produced by '''boiling seawater''' since salt-field technology had not yet developed in Japan. After '''salt-field techniques''' became established, the field was constructed in western Futami Town as an '''agehama-style salt field''', though the exact date of its establishment is unknown. Later, it was converted into an '''irihama-style salt field'''.
Due to '''coastal erosion''' and '''land subsidence''', maintenance became difficult, and in 1751 (*Kan'en 4*), embankment work was carried out to preserve the field. However, as maintaining the original location was still challenging, the salt field was moved to its '''current location near the Isuzu River estuary'''.
During the '''Meiji Restoration''', the new government confiscated Mishiohama under the '''Land Requisition Order''', ceasing salt production. However, in 1877 (*Meiji 10*), the Jingū purchased the land, and in 1897 (*Meiji 30*), salt production resumed.
== Mishio-Kumiire-sho ==
[[File:Mishioden-jinja_05.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishio-Kumiire-sho]] The '''Mishio-Kumiire-sho''' (*御塩汲入所*) is a storage facility for brine (*kansui*), which is transported from Mishiohama in barrels. It is located in the eastern part of the shrine grounds near the coast.
The structure is built in the '''Tenchi-Kongen style''' (*天地根元造*), once thought to represent a '''primitive dwelling style''', but later identified as a '''post-classical invention''' rather than an ancient form.
== Mishio-Yakisho ==
[[File:Mishioden-jinja_04.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishio-Yakisho]] The '''Mishio-Yakisho''' (*御塩焼所*) is where the '''brine is boiled in iron kettles''' to produce '''crude salt''' (*arashio*). It is slightly larger than the Kumiire-sho and shares the same '''Tenchi-Kongen construction'''. This process, using '''ritual fire''' (*imibi*), occurs in '''August'''.
== Mishio-Mikura ==
[[File:Mishioden-jinja_08.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishio-Mikura]] The '''Mishio-Mikura''' (*御塩御倉*) is a storage warehouse where '''crude salt is kept''' before processing in the Mishioden.
== Mishioden ==
[[File:Mishio-den-jinja_01.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishioden]] The '''Mishioden''' (*御塩殿*) is the '''facility where hardened salt is made''' by '''firing the crude salt''' in triangular '''earthenware molds''' (*doki*). The process uses ritual fire (*imibi*) and is performed twice a year in '''October and March'''.
== Mishio-michi ==
The '''Mishio-michi''' (*御塩道*) is the designated route for '''transporting the hardened salt''' from Mishioden Shrine to the Jingū. The historical route passed through modern locations such as Futami Town, Kurose-chō, and Kawazaki, before reaching the '''Outer Shrine''' (*Gekū*). Originally carried by hand, transportation switched to '''vehicles''' in the '''Shōwa era'''.
== deity ==
The shrine deity is '''Mishioden Chinju-no-Kami''', though some theories suggest it may originally have been {{ill|Shiotsuchi-no-Oji|ja|シオツチノオジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiotsuchi-no-Oji|qq=}}.
== History == The exact origins are unclear, but the ''{{ill|Enryaku Gishikichō|ja|延暦儀式帳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryaku Gishikichō|qq=}}'' from 804 (Enryaku 23) mentions the {{ill|Mishiodono|ja|御塩殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mishiodono|qq=}} and {{ill|Mishioyaki-dono|ja|御塩焼殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mishioyaki-dono|qq=}}. During the 10th–11th centuries in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the area around Mishiodono was developed into the {{ill|Futami-no-shō|ja|二見庄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futami-no-shō|qq=}}, which became part of the outer shrine’s territory (*shinryō*, or Gekū domain).
From 1486 (Bunmei 18) during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] until the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Futami was under the control of the warrior class, making it difficult to manage Mishiodono. In the early Edo period, the {{ill|Kuki clan|ja|九鬼家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuki clan|qq=}}, lords of the [[:en:Toba Domain|Toba Domain]], faced internal succession disputes, leading to their dispossession. In 1632 (Kan'ei 10), Futami was officially restored as shrine territory (*shinryō*).
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the new government issued the '''{{ill|Edict for the Confiscation of Shrine and Temple Lands|ja|上知令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edict for the Confiscation of Shrine and Temple Lands|qq=}}''' in January 1871 (Meiji 4), confiscating all shrine and temple lands except for their immediate precincts, effectively abolishing the shrine land system. As a result, Mishiohama was confiscated, and the production of coarse salt ceased. The [[:en:List of Jingū|Ise Grand Shrine]] had to purchase coarse salt from other sources, which was then roasted and hardened at Mishiodono for ritual use.
In 1877 (Meiji 10), the shrine repurchased the land of Mishiohama, and by 1897 (Meiji 30), the traditional production of sacred salt (*mishio chōshin*) resumed at Mishiohama and {{ill|Mishioden Shrine|ja|御塩殿神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mishioden Shrine|qq=}}. The tradition of having former shrine domain residents participate in the salt-making process has been preserved ever since.
== festival ==
The '''Mishioden Festival''' (*御塩殿祭*) is held annually on '''October 5th''', praying for '''successful salt production''' and the '''safety of workers'''.
== Transportation ==
* '''Train''': 1.2 km from [[:en:Futaminoura Station|Futamiura Station]] * '''Bus''': 900m from the [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] Futamiura stop * '''Car''': 2.3 km from the {{ill|Ise Futami Toba Toll Road|ja|伊勢二見鳥羽有料道路|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Futami Toba Toll Road|qq=}}
== See Also == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] * [[:en:Shinsen|Shinsen (food offerings)]] * [[:en:Salt evaporation pond|Salt field]] * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|qq=}} * [[:en:Futami, Mie|Futami, Mie]]
== 交通 == * 最寄駅：[[東海旅客鉄道|JR東海]][[参宮線]][[二見浦駅]]から約1.2km。 * 最寄バス停：[[三重交通]]二見浦表参道バス停下車約900m。 * 最寄インターチェンジ：[[伊勢二見鳥羽有料道路]][[二見TB|二見料金所]]から約2.3km。 * 駐車場：二見グラウンド東隣の二見浦海水浴場の駐車場より徒歩約400m、二見生涯学習センター裏の駐車場より徒歩約800m。

[[ja:御塩殿神社]] [[sn:Mishioden Shrine]] [[sn:ishioden Shrine]]
<!--Mishioden Shrine--> [[Category:二見町]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Mishioden-jinja_02.JPG|256px|Mishioden Shrine]] | name = Mishioden Shrine (御塩殿神社) | location = Futami-chō Shō, [[Ise, Mie]] | deity = Mishioden Chinju-no-Kami | Rank = {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} subsidiary shrine | established = Before [[804]] | main_hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | festival = Mishioden Festival }}
'''Mishioden Shrine''' (御塩殿神社) is a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} located in [[:en:Futami, Mie|Futami-chō, Mie]], [[Ise, Mie]]. The shrine houses facilities for producing hardened salt (*katashio*), which is an essential offering in Shinto rituals. Within the Ise Grand Shrine (*Jingū*), the official reading of the shrine's name is "Mishiodono," while locals in Futami refer to it as "Mishioden."
== Overview ==
[[File:Mishioden-jinja_10.JPG|thumb|240px|Mishioden Shrine grounds]] '''Mishioden Shrine''' is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] that includes facilities for producing hardened salt used in the sacred food offerings (*shinsen*) of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]. It is located in Futami-chō Shō, Ise, Mie, covering an area of 27,785 [[square meters]] (*m²*). The name "Shō" originates from *Futami-no-Shō*, a land developed for the Jingū during the [[Heian period]].
Originally, the shrine had no formal structure, and the deity was enshrined within the '''Mishioden''', a facility where salt was processed.
Outside the shrine precincts, to the west of Futami Town, lies the '''Mishiohama salt field''' (*御塩浜*), while within the shrine grounds are facilities such as the '''Mishio-Kumiire-sho''' (*御塩汲入所*), '''Mishio-Yakisho''' (*御塩焼所*), '''Mishio-Mikura''' (*御塩御倉*), and '''Mishioden''' (*御塩殿*). These facilities are maintained through the labor of local residents.
Although Mishioden Shrine is under the jurisdiction of the '''Inner Shrine''' (*Naikū*), it also supplies hardened salt for use in rituals at the '''Outer Shrine''' (*Gekū*). One theory suggests that a historical mix-up with {{ill|Katada Shrine|ja|堅田神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katada Shrine|qq=}} led to the Gekū receiving salt from Mishioden.
Historically, salt production flourished along the '''Ise Bay''' coastline from Futami-chō to {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōminato, Ise|qq=}}, but it declined with the rise of '''irihama-style salt fields''' in the Seto Inland Sea. Today, Mishiohama is the last remaining salt field in this region.
In addition to Mishioden Shrine, other shrines classified as '''Imperial Estate Shrines''' (*Goryōchi shrines*) responsible for producing ritual offerings include the {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|ja|機殿神社|lt='''Kanfusoki Hatadono Shrine'''|lt_ja=神服織機殿神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatadono Shrines|qq=}} (*神服織機殿神社*), which weaves silk, and the {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|ja|機殿神社|lt='''Kanmisoki Hatadono Shrine'''|lt_ja=神麻続機殿神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatadono Shrines|qq=}} (*神麻続機殿神社*), which produces hemp fabric. Both are located in [[:en:Matsusaka|Matsusaka, Mie]].

Historically, salt production flourished along the '''Ise Bay''' coastline from Futami-chō to {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōminato, Ise|qq=}}, but it declined with the rise of '''irihama-style salt fields''' in the Seto Inland Sea. Today, Mishiohama is the last remaining salt field in this region.
In addition to Mishioden Shrine, other shrines classified as '''Imperial Estate Shrines''' (*Goryōchi shrines*) responsible for producing ritual offerings include the {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|ja|機殿神社|lt='''Kanfusoki Hatadono Shrine'''|lt_ja=神服織機殿神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatadono Shrines|qq=}} (*神服織機殿神社*), which weaves silk, and the {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|ja|機殿神社|lt='''Kanmisoki Hatadono Shrine'''|lt_ja=神麻続機殿神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatadono Shrines|qq=}} (*神麻続機殿神社*), which produces hemp fabric. Both are located in [[:en:Matsusaka|Matsusaka, Mie]].
== Mishiohama ==
[[File:Mishiohama 01.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishiohama salt field]] [[File:Isuzu-gawa 01.JPG|thumb|160px|Isuzu River near Mishiohama]] [[File:Mishiohama 02.JPG|thumb|160px|Water gate at Mishiohama]]
The '''Mishiohama salt field''' (*御塩浜*) is a 6,609 *m²* salt field associated with the shrine. Although it is not a shrine itself, it features a '''blackwood torii gate'''.
Originally, salt was produced by '''boiling seawater''' since salt-field technology had not yet developed in Japan. After '''salt-field techniques''' became established, the field was constructed in western Futami Town as an '''agehama-style salt field''', though the exact date of its establishment is unknown. Later, it was converted into an '''irihama-style salt field'''.
Due to '''coastal erosion''' and '''land subsidence''', maintenance became difficult, and in 1751 (*Kan'en 4*), embankment work was carried out to preserve the field. However, as maintaining the original location was still challenging, the salt field was moved to its '''current location near the Isuzu River estuary'''.
During the '''Meiji Restoration''', the new government confiscated Mishiohama under the '''Land Requisition Order''', ceasing salt production. However, in 1877 (*Meiji 10*), the Jingū purchased the land, and in 1897 (*Meiji 30*), salt production resumed.
== Mishio-Kumiire-sho ==
[[File:Mishioden-jinja_05.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishio-Kumiire-sho]] The '''Mishio-Kumiire-sho''' (*御塩汲入所*) is a storage facility for brine (*kansui*), which is transported from Mishiohama in barrels. It is located in the eastern part of the shrine grounds near the coast.
The structure is built in the '''Tenchi-Kongen style''' (*天地根元造*), once thought to represent a '''primitive dwelling style''', but later identified as a '''post-classical invention''' rather than an ancient form.
== Mishio-Yakisho ==
[[File:Mishioden-jinja_04.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishio-Yakisho]] The '''Mishio-Yakisho''' (*御塩焼所*) is where the '''brine is boiled in iron kettles''' to produce '''crude salt''' (*arashio*). It is slightly larger than the Kumiire-sho and shares the same '''Tenchi-Kongen construction'''. This process, using '''ritual fire''' (*imibi*), occurs in '''August'''.
== Mishio-Mikura ==
[[File:Mishioden-jinja_08.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishio-Mikura]] The '''Mishio-Mikura''' (*御塩御倉*) is a storage warehouse where '''crude salt is kept''' before processing in the Mishioden.
== Mishioden ==
[[File:Mishio-den-jinja_01.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishioden]] The '''Mishioden''' (*御塩殿*) is the '''facility where hardened salt is made''' by '''firing the crude salt''' in triangular '''earthenware molds''' (*doki*). The process uses ritual fire (*imibi*) and is performed twice a year in '''October and March'''.
== Mishio-michi ==
The '''Mishio-michi''' (*御塩道*) is the designated route for '''transporting the hardened salt''' from Mishioden Shrine to the Jingū. The historical route passed through modern locations such as Futami Town, Kurose-chō, and Kawazaki, before reaching the '''Outer Shrine''' (*Gekū*). Originally carried by hand, transportation switched to '''vehicles''' in the '''Shōwa era'''.
== deity ==
The shrine deity is '''Mishioden Chinju-no-Kami''', though some theories suggest it may originally have been {{ill|Shiotsuchi-no-Oji|ja|シオツチノオジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiotsuchi-no-Oji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シオツチオジ}}.
== History == The exact origins are unclear, but the ''{{ill|Enryaku Gishikichō|ja|延暦儀式帳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryaku Gishikichō|qq=}}'' from 804 (Enryaku 23) mentions the {{ill|Mishiodono|ja|御塩殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mishiodono|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Mishioyaki-dono|ja|御塩焼殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mishioyaki-dono|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. During the 10th–11th centuries in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the area around Mishiodono was developed into the {{ill|Futami-no-shō|ja|二見庄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futami-no-shō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which became part of the outer shrine’s territory (*shinryō*, or Gekū domain).
From 1486 (Bunmei 18) during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] until the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Futami was under the control of the warrior class, making it difficult to manage Mishiodono. In the early Edo period, the {{ill|Kuki clan|ja|九鬼家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuki clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九鬼氏}}, lords of the [[:en:Toba Domain|Toba Domain]], faced internal succession disputes, leading to their dispossession. In 1632 (Kan'ei 10), Futami was officially restored as shrine territory (*shinryō*).
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the new government issued the '''{{ill|Edict for the Confiscation of Shrine and Temple Lands|ja|上知令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edict for the Confiscation of Shrine and Temple Lands|qq=}}''' in January 1871 (Meiji 4), confiscating all shrine and temple lands except for their immediate precincts, effectively abolishing the shrine land system. As a result, Mishiohama was confiscated, and the production of coarse salt ceased. The [[:en:List of Jingū|Ise Grand Shrine]] had to purchase coarse salt from other sources, which was then roasted and hardened at Mishiodono for ritual use.
In 1877 (Meiji 10), the shrine repurchased the land of Mishiohama, and by 1897 (Meiji 30), the traditional production of sacred salt (*mishio chōshin*) resumed at Mishiohama and {{ill|Mishioden Shrine|ja|御塩殿神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mishioden Shrine|qq=}}. The tradition of having former shrine domain residents participate in the salt-making process has been preserved ever since.
== festival ==
The '''Mishioden Festival''' (*御塩殿祭*) is held annually on '''October 5th''', praying for '''successful salt production''' and the '''safety of workers'''.
== Transportation ==
* '''Train''': 1.2 km from [[:en:Futaminoura Station|Futamiura Station]] * '''Bus''': 900m from the [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] Futamiura stop * '''Car''': 2.3 km from the {{ill|Ise Futami Toba Toll Road|ja|伊勢二見鳥羽有料道路|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Futami Toba Toll Road|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢二見鳥羽ライン}}
== See Also == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] * [[:en:Shinsen|Shinsen (food offerings)]] * [[:en:Salt evaporation pond|Salt field]] * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢神宮}} * [[:en:Futami, Mie|Futami, Mie]]
== 交通 == * 最寄駅：[[東海旅客鉄道|JR東海]][[参宮線]][[二見浦駅]]から約1.2km。 * 最寄バス停：[[三重交通]]二見浦表参道バス停下車約900m。 * 最寄インターチェンジ：[[伊勢二見鳥羽有料道路]][[二見TB|二見料金所]]から約2.3km。 * 駐車場：二見グラウンド東隣の二見浦海水浴場の駐車場より徒歩約400m、二見生涯学習センター裏の駐車場より徒歩約800m。
== 参考資料 == {{No footnotes|date=2019th year 10月|section=1}} * 『二見町史』（昭和63rd year 3月31日発行、編集：二見町史編纂委員会、発行：二見町役場） * 『伊勢神宮の祖型と展開』 （平成3rd year 11月30日発行、桜井勝之進著、国書刊行会発行、ISBN 4-336-03296-3） * 『お伊勢まいり』（平成18th year 7月1日発行改訂7版、伊勢神宮崇敬会）
*
== External Links == {{commonscat|Mishioden-jinja}} * [http://www.ise-kanko.jp/base_data/base_data.php?info_cd=00336 伊勢市観光協会／御塩殿神社] * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m10/1001.html 御塩殿神社（みしおどのじんじゃ）] - 伊勢神宮崇敬会

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Mishioden-jinja_02.JPG|256px|Mishioden Shrine]] | name = Mishioden Shrine (御塩殿神社) | location = Futami-chō Shō, [[Ise, Mie]] | deity = Mishioden Chinju-no-Kami | Rank = {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} subsidiary shrine | established = Before [[:en:804|804]] | main_hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | festival = Mishioden Festival }}
'''Mishioden Shrine''' (御塩殿神社) is a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} located in [[:en:Futami, Mie|Futami-chō, Mie]], [[Ise, Mie]]. The shrine houses facilities for producing hardened salt (*katashio*), which is an essential offering in Shinto rituals. Within the Ise Grand Shrine (*Jingū*), the official reading of the shrine's name is "Mishiodono," while locals in Futami refer to it as "Mishioden."
== Overview ==
[[File:Mishioden-jinja_10.JPG|thumb|240px|Mishioden Shrine grounds]] '''Mishioden Shrine''' is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] that includes facilities for producing hardened salt used in the sacred food offerings (*shinsen*) of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]. It is located in Futami-chō Shō, Ise, Mie, covering an area of 27,785 [[square meters]] (*m²*). The name "Shō" originates from *Futami-no-Shō*, a land developed for the Jingū during the [[Heian period]].
Originally, the shrine had no formal structure, and the deity was enshrined within the '''Mishioden''', a facility where salt was processed.
Outside the shrine precincts, to the west of Futami Town, lies the '''Mishiohama salt field''' (*御塩浜*), while within the shrine grounds are facilities such as the '''Mishio-Kumiire-sho''' (*御塩汲入所*), '''Mishio-Yakisho''' (*御塩焼所*), '''Mishio-Mikura''' (*御塩御倉*), and '''Mishioden''' (*御塩殿*). These facilities are maintained through the labor of local residents.
Although Mishioden Shrine is under the jurisdiction of the '''Inner Shrine''' (*Naikū*), it also supplies hardened salt for use in rituals at the '''Outer Shrine''' (*Gekū*). One theory suggests that a historical mix-up with {{ill|Katada Shrine|ja|堅田神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katada Shrine|qq=}} led to the Gekū receiving salt from Mishioden.
Historically, salt production flourished along the '''Ise Bay''' coastline from Futami-chō to {{ill|Ōminato, Ise|ja|大湊 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōminato, Ise|qq=}}, but it declined with the rise of '''irihama-style salt fields''' in the Seto Inland Sea. Today, Mishiohama is the last remaining salt field in this region.
In addition to Mishioden Shrine, other shrines classified as '''Imperial Estate Shrines''' (*Goryōchi shrines*) responsible for producing ritual offerings include the {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|ja|機殿神社|lt='''Kanfusoki Hatadono Shrine'''|lt_ja=神服織機殿神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatadono Shrines|qq=}} (*神服織機殿神社*), which weaves silk, and the {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|ja|機殿神社|lt='''Kanmisoki Hatadono Shrine'''|lt_ja=神麻続機殿神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatadono Shrines|qq=}} (*神麻続機殿神社*), which produces hemp fabric. Both are located in [[:en:Matsusaka|Matsusaka, Mie]].
== Mishiohama ==
[[File:Mishiohama 01.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishiohama salt field]] [[File:Isuzu-gawa 01.JPG|thumb|160px|Isuzu River near Mishiohama]] [[File:Mishiohama 02.JPG|thumb|160px|Water gate at Mishiohama]]
The '''Mishiohama salt field''' (*御塩浜*) is a 6,609 *m²* salt field associated with the shrine. Although it is not a shrine itself, it features a '''blackwood torii gate'''.
Originally, salt was produced by '''boiling seawater''' since salt-field technology had not yet developed in Japan. After '''salt-field techniques''' became established, the field was constructed in western Futami Town as an '''agehama-style salt field''', though the exact date of its establishment is unknown. Later, it was converted into an '''irihama-style salt field'''.
Due to '''coastal erosion''' and '''land subsidence''', maintenance became difficult, and in 1751 (*Kan'en 4*), embankment work was carried out to preserve the field. However, as maintaining the original location was still challenging, the salt field was moved to its '''current location near the Isuzu River estuary'''.
During the '''Meiji Restoration''', the new government confiscated Mishiohama under the '''Land Requisition Order''', ceasing salt production. However, in 1877 (*Meiji 10*), the Jingū purchased the land, and in 1897 (*Meiji 30*), salt production resumed.
== Mishio-Kumiire-sho ==
[[File:Mishioden-jinja_05.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishio-Kumiire-sho]] The '''Mishio-Kumiire-sho''' (*御塩汲入所*) is a storage facility for brine (*kansui*), which is transported from Mishiohama in barrels. It is located in the eastern part of the shrine grounds near the coast.
The structure is built in the '''Tenchi-Kongen style''' (*天地根元造*), once thought to represent a '''primitive dwelling style''', but later identified as a '''post-classical invention''' rather than an ancient form.
== Mishio-Yakisho ==
[[File:Mishioden-jinja_04.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishio-Yakisho]] The '''Mishio-Yakisho''' (*御塩焼所*) is where the '''brine is boiled in iron kettles''' to produce '''crude salt''' (*arashio*). It is slightly larger than the Kumiire-sho and shares the same '''Tenchi-Kongen construction'''. This process, using '''ritual fire''' (*imibi*), occurs in '''August'''.
== Mishio-Mikura ==
[[File:Mishioden-jinja_08.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishio-Mikura]] The '''Mishio-Mikura''' (*御塩御倉*) is a storage warehouse where '''crude salt is kept''' before processing in the Mishioden.
== Mishioden ==
[[File:Mishio-den-jinja_01.JPG|thumb|160px|Mishioden]] The '''Mishioden''' (*御塩殿*) is the '''facility where hardened salt is made''' by '''firing the crude salt''' in triangular '''earthenware molds''' (*doki*). The process uses ritual fire (*imibi*) and is performed twice a year in '''October and March'''.
== Mishio-michi ==
The '''Mishio-michi''' (*御塩道*) is the designated route for '''transporting the hardened salt''' from Mishioden Shrine to the Jingū. The historical route passed through modern locations such as Futami Town, Kurose-chō, and Kawazaki, before reaching the '''Outer Shrine''' (*Gekū*). Originally carried by hand, transportation switched to '''vehicles''' in the '''Shōwa era'''.
== deity ==
The shrine deity is '''Mishioden Chinju-no-Kami''', though some theories suggest it may originally have been {{ill|Shiotsuchi-no-Oji|ja|シオツチノオジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiotsuchi-no-Oji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シオツチオジ}}.
== History == The exact origins are unclear, but the ''{{ill|Enryaku Gishikichō|ja|延暦儀式帳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryaku Gishikichō|qq=}}'' from 804 (Enryaku 23) mentions the {{ill|Mishiodono|ja|御塩殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mishiodono|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Mishioyaki-dono|ja|御塩焼殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mishioyaki-dono|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. During the 10th–11th centuries in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the area around Mishiodono was developed into the {{ill|Futami-no-shō|ja|二見庄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futami-no-shō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which became part of the outer shrine’s territory (*shinryō*, or Gekū domain).

In the early Edo period, the {{ill|Kuki clan|ja|九鬼家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuki clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九鬼氏}}, lords of the [[:en:Toba Domain|Toba Domain]], faced internal succession disputes, leading to their dispossession. In 1632 (Kan'ei 10), Futami was officially restored as shrine territory (*shinryō*).
After the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the new government issued the '''{{ill|Edict for the Confiscation of Shrine and Temple Lands|ja|上知令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edict for the Confiscation of Shrine and Temple Lands|qq=}}''' in January 1871 (Meiji 4), confiscating all shrine and temple lands except for their immediate precincts, effectively abolishing the shrine land system. As a result, Mishiohama was confiscated, and the production of coarse salt ceased. The [[:en:List of Jingū|Ise Grand Shrine]] had to purchase coarse salt from other sources, which was then roasted and hardened at Mishiodono for ritual use.
In 1877 (Meiji 10), the shrine repurchased the land of Mishiohama, and by 1897 (Meiji 30), the traditional production of sacred salt (*mishio chōshin*) resumed at Mishiohama and {{ill|Mishioden Shrine|ja|御塩殿神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mishioden Shrine|qq=}}. The tradition of having former shrine domain residents participate in the salt-making process has been preserved ever since.
== festival ==
The '''Mishioden Festival''' (*御塩殿祭*) is held annually on '''October 5th''', praying for '''successful salt production''' and the '''safety of workers'''.
== Transportation ==
* '''Train''': 1.2 km from [[:en:Futaminoura Station|Futamiura Station]] * '''Bus''': 900m from the [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] Futamiura stop * '''Car''': 2.3 km from the {{ill|Ise Futami Toba Toll Road|ja|伊勢二見鳥羽有料道路|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Futami Toba Toll Road|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢二見鳥羽ライン}}
